cases.name: html_renders_dynamic_template
cases.note: GET /html renders a server-side template interpolating a name, a list, and a number. Frameworks' template engines differ in whitespace/tag layout, so the contract asserts text/html + the presence of each interpolated value (htmlContains, whitespace-tolerant) rather than a byte-exact body. CANON values: greeting 'Hello, Alice', list=[apple,banana,cherry], and the labeled number 'Total: 42' — labeled so the assertion cannot collide with an incidental 42 elsewhere in the markup.

cases.name: jwt_sign_then_verify
cases.note: CANON: GET /jwt/sign issues an HS256 token with fixed claims (sub=1234567890, name=John Doe, admin=true) plus iat+exp, signed with the shared JWT_SECRET. $jwt verifies the signature cryptographically against that secret and requires an unexpired exp (golang-jwt). The verify step then decodes the SAME token and echoes the payload; iat/exp are dynamic ($number), the rest are asserted literally.

cases.name: jwt_verify_bad_signature_401
cases.note: SECURITY: a structurally-valid HS256 token signed with a DIFFERENT secret must be rejected — proves the server verifies the signature, not just parses the token. This token is signed with a throwaway secret (not JWT_SECRET), so it is stable regardless of the shared secret value.

cases.name: jwt_verify_expired_token_401
cases.note: SECURITY (PLAN §8: tampered/expired token → 401): this token is signed with the CORRECT dev-default JWT_SECRET but its exp is 2020-01-01T01:00:00Z — a server that verifies the signature but ignores exp would wrongly return 200. Statically embedded; only valid against the dev-default secret, which is what the contract harness always runs with.

cases.name: jwt_verify_none_alg_401
cases.note: SECURITY (alg confusion): an UNSIGNED token with header {"alg":"none"} and an empty signature segment must be rejected. It carries the canon claims and a far-future exp (4102444800), so a server that trusts the header's alg and skips verification would wrongly return 200. Every stack pins HS256 at the library-config level (golang-jwt WithValidMethods, jose alg, PyJWT algorithms, jsonwebtoken Validation, auth0 HMAC256, Zig hand-rolled pinning), so alg=none is refused. Statically embedded (no signature to compute).

cases.name: validate_accepts_valid_object
cases.note: CANON /validate schema (~4 levels): user{ id:uuid, email:email, profile{ age:0..120, role:admin|user|guest, preferences{ theme:light|dark, notifications:bool } } }, items[]{ sku:string, quantity:1..100, tags:string[] }, total:number>=0. A fully valid object returns 200 {"valid":true}.

cases.name: validate_rejects_invalid_object
cases.note: The mirror-image object with a broken uuid, bad email, out-of-range age, invalid role enum, out-of-range quantity, bad theme enum, and negative total. Returns a 400 with the canonical error shape; details carries a per-framework summary of the failures (asserted $present, not an exact count — Zod/Pydantic/validator/serde count and word failures differently).

cases.name: validate_omitted_age_200
cases.note: ZERO-VALUE CANON: age is NOT required — omitting it entirely defaults to 0, which satisfies the 0..120 range (Go: int zero-value 0; the rule is gte=0,lte=120 without required). A server whose validator treats a missing optional field as an error would wrongly 400. The rest of the object is the fully-valid shape.

cases.name: validate_omitted_total_200
cases.note: ZERO-VALUE CANON: total is NOT required — omitting it defaults to 0, which satisfies gte=0. Only the presence of the field is optional; its zero value is a legal amount.

cases.name: validate_omitted_tags_200
cases.note: ZERO-VALUE CANON: tags is NOT required and carries no presence rule — omitting it from an item defaults to an empty/nil list, which is valid. (Distinct from the sku/quantity fields on the same item, which ARE required and range-checked.)

cases.name: validate_empty_items_400
cases.note: BOUNDARY CANON: items has a min=1 rule — an empty array is a violation (an order with no line items). Returns the canonical validation-failed error, same shape as validate_rejects_invalid_object.

cases.name: validate_empty_sku_400
cases.note: BOUNDARY CANON: sku is required — an empty string fails the required rule (empty string is the zero value, which 'required' rejects). Everything else on the object is valid, isolating the sku violation.

cases.name: compute_one_round
cases.note: CANON: GET /compute?n= applies SHA-256 n times to the fixed seed bytes "benchmark" and returns the lowercase hex digest. $sha256chain:<n> recomputes it independently in the runner. n must be an integer >= 1 (validate-at-boundary): missing, non-numeric, zero, or negative n is a 400; above the cap it is clamped.

cases.name: compute_caps_excessive_n
cases.note: CANON: n above the cap (1000000) is CLAMPED, not rejected — the response is still 200 and equals the cap-rounds chain. This bounds the per-request CPU work.

cases.name: compute_missing_n_400
cases.note: CANON: n is required (validate-at-boundary — unlike /params/search's limit, which has a documented default to fall back to, /compute has no meaningful default amount of work). Error string follows the house 'invalid <thing>' style (shared consts: invalid JSON body / invalid form data / invalid token); details $present like every other 400.

cases.name: compute_zero_n_400
cases.note: n must be >= 1: zero rounds would return the raw seed digest-free, which is no compute at all.

cases.name: compute_underscore_n_400
cases.note: PARSE CANON (Go strconv.Atoi): digit-group underscores like 1_000 are a source-literal convenience, NOT valid input — Atoi rejects them. Zig's std.fmt.parseInt ACCEPTS underscores by default, so this pins the Go behavior as canon.

cases.name: compute_whitespace_n_400
cases.note: PARSE CANON (Go strconv.Atoi): a leading space is rejected — Atoi does not trim. The query value is a literal space + '5'; the runner url-encodes it and the server must see the untrimmed value and reject it.

cases.name: compute_unicode_digit_n_400
cases.note: PARSE CANON (Go strconv.Atoi): only ASCII digits count. This is the Arabic-Indic digit five U+0665 (٥), which Python int() and some parsers accept as numeric — Atoi rejects it. Pins ASCII-only parsing as canon.

cases.name: compute_plus_n_200
cases.note: PARSE CANON (Go strconv.Atoi): a leading '+' sign is accepted (Atoi handles optional +/-). '+5' parses to 5. The runner url-encodes the '+' as %2B so it reaches the server as a literal plus, not a space.

cases.name: compute_leading_zeros_n_200
cases.note: PARSE CANON (Go strconv.Atoi): leading zeros are decimal, NOT octal — '007' parses to 7 (not 7 octal, which is coincidentally also 7, but e.g. '010' would be 10 decimal). Pins base-10 parsing.

cases.name: compute_int64_overflow_n_400
cases.note: PARSE CANON (Go strconv.Atoi = signed 64-bit): 9300000000000000000 exceeds i64 max (9223372036854775807) but is below u64 max, so a server parsing n as UNSIGNED 64-bit would accept it (then clamp) and wrongly return 200. Canon parses as signed i64: this overflows and is a 400. Paired with compute_huge_n_clamped_200 to pin both sides of the i64 boundary.

cases.name: compute_huge_n_clamped_200
cases.note: PARSE CANON (the other side of the i64 boundary): 9007199254740993 (2^53+1) is a valid i64, far above the 1000000 cap, so it is CLAMPED to the cap and returns 200 with the cap-rounds digest. This stops a server from passing compute_int64_overflow_n_400 by rejecting large-but-valid values too early — the boundary is i64 max, not the cap.
